title: Petroleum facility access control

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run, then normalised in Phase A (clause 'Petroleumsforskriften' → '§15'). Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

dataSources: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621)
spl:
index=pacs sourcetype="badge:swipe" site="NOR-OIL-*" earliest=-24h
| eval day=strftime(_time,"%Y-%m-%d"), user=lower(user)
| stats earliest(_time) as first_in, latest(_time) as last_in by user, day, site
| join type=left user, day [| search index=vpn sourcetype="paloalto:globalprotect" log_type="connect" earliest=-2d
    | eval day=strftime(_time,"%Y-%m-%d"), user=lower(user)
    | stats dc(public_ip) as vpn_ips by user, day]
| where isnull(vpn_ips) AND (last_in-first_in)>3600
| table user, day, site, first_in, last_in, vpn_ips
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user | sort - count
description: Correlates physical badge events with logical sessions into engineering workstations for facility access control.
value: Correlates physical badge events with logical sessions into engineering workstations for facility access control.
implementation: (1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain supporting lookups (classified hosts, sectors, transfers) aligned to Norwegian regulatory language. (2) Schedule searches with Europe/Oslo time semantics for reporting cutoffs. (3) Route ES notables to the correct national security, OT, or privacy queues. (4) Quarterly false-positive tuning with HR, operations, and legal stakeholders.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=pacs sourcetype="badge:swipe" site="NOR-OIL-*" earliest=-24h
| eval day=strftime(_time,"%Y-%m-%d"), user=lower(user)
| stats earliest(_time) as first_in, latest(_time) as last_in by user, day, site
| join type=left user, day [| search index=vpn sourcetype="paloalto:globalprotect" log_type="connect" earliest=-2d
    | eval day=strftime(_time,"%Y-%m-%d"), user=lower(user)
    | stats dc(public_ip) as vpn_ips by user, day]
| where isnull(vpn_ips) AND (last_in-first_in)>3600
| table user, day, site, first_in, last_in, vpn_ips
```

Understanding this SPL

**Petroleum facility access control (Petroleumsforskriften)** — Correlates physical badge events with logical sessions into engineering workstations for facility access control.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: pacs; **sourcetype**: badge:swipe. If that sourcetype is not mentioned in Data sources, double-check parsing or update the documentation to match the feed you actually ingest.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=pacs, sourcetype="badge:swipe", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **day** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by user, day, site** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Joins to a subsearch with `join` — set `max=` to match cardinality and avoid silent truncation.
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(vpn_ips) AND (last_in-first_in)>3600` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Pipeline stage (see **Petroleum facility access control (Petroleumsforskriften)**): table user, day, site, first_in, last_in, vpn_ips

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.user | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Petroleum facility access control (Petroleumsforskriften)** — Correlates physical badge events with logical sessions into engineering workstations for facility access control.

Documented **Data sources**: Windows Security Event Logs, network infrastructure logs, OT/ICS system logs, power grid SCADA, petroleum control systems, government system audit trails, identity management systems, security incident reports. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux (833), Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk ITSI (1841), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).
visualization: Bar chart (events by host), Table (top users), Single value (policy violations).

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Unix and Linux

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
